suggested.title: [Look Together] Coins and currency from different countries

competence_markers.description: The child can explain in their own words why money was invented and what problem it solves
The child can correctly count coins and make change for amounts under $20
The child can identify at least three properties of good money (durable, portable, divisible, scarce, accepted)
The child can explain the difference between earning and receiving money
The child can explain opportunity cost using a real example from their own experience
The child can distinguish between needs and wants with real examples
The child can describe what makes a trade fair and why trust matters in exchange
The child has handled real money in real transactions (at a store, at a yard sale, or in a trading exercise)

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of money.
narration_prompts.detail: Why did people invent it? What problem did it solve? What did people use as money before coins and bills?
Why is a glass of water almost free at home but incredibly valuable in a desert? What makes something valuable?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between earning money and receiving money?
narration_prompts.detail: Why does earning teach you something that receiving does not?
Tell me about a time you had to choose between two things you wanted. What did you choose, and what did you give up?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between a need and a want?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me three examples of each. Can something be a need for one person and a want for another?
Why does fairness matter in buying and selling?

cross_connections.description: Counting money, making change, and understanding denominations are direct applications of the number sense developed in 1.5.1. The child practicing money math is reinforcing quantitative skills in the most practical context available.
The discipline of saving, the ability to delay gratification, and the honesty required for fair exchange are all expressions of the character foundation built in IC1.1.
Household purchasing decisions, meal planning on a budget, and understanding the cost of running a home all connect to the money awareness begun here.
The invention of money is part of the story of civilization. The child who understands why trade networks formed and why cities grew is learning the same story from a different angle.
The spending and saving habits introduced here become the formal budgeting skills of 4.1.2. This packet lays the conceptual foundation; 4.1.2 builds the operational practice.
The concept that value comes from solving problems is the central idea of 4.1.4. The child who understands value creation here is ready to begin actually creating value through a simple business.

content.why_this_matters:
Have you ever wondered why people use money? Why can you not just walk into a store and take what you need? Why do some things cost more than others? Why do your parents go to work every day?

Money is one of the most important inventions in all of human history — as important as writing, as important as the wheel. Before money existed, people had to trade things directly with each other. If a farmer wanted a new pair of shoes, she had to find a shoemaker who wanted eggs. If the shoemaker did not want eggs, the farmer was out of luck. Money solved this problem. It let anyone trade with anyone for anything. That single invention changed the world.

In this packet, you are going to learn what money actually is, where value comes from, and how exchange works. You will handle real money, make real decisions about spending and saving, and begin to understand something that will matter for your entire life: **money is a tool, and the person who understands how the tool works has power that the person who does not understand it will never have.**

You will also learn something even more important than money itself: **value comes from solving problems for other people.** The person who can see what others need and provide it — that person will never struggle to earn money, because money flows toward people who create value.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Concrete understanding of what money is** — not as an abstraction, but as a real tool that people invented to solve a real problem
2. **The concept of value** — that things are worth something because they solve problems or meet needs, not because of some inherent magic
3. **The ability to handle real money** — counting coins, making change, understanding denominations
4. **The difference between earning and receiving** — and why earning teaches something that receiving cannot
5. **The habit of choosing** — understanding that spending money on one thing means not having it for another (opportunity cost)
6. **The distinction between needs and wants** — not as a moral judgment but as a practical framework for making decisions
7. **The foundation of fair exchange** — that good trades make both people happier
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Before Money — The Problem of Trading

Imagine you are a farmer. You grow apples — lots of apples. You have more apples than your family can eat. But what you really need is a warm wool blanket because winter is coming.

You know a weaver who makes beautiful blankets. So you go to the weaver and say, "I will trade you a basket of my best apples for one of your blankets."

But the weaver says, "I do not want apples. I have plenty of food. What I need is a new clay pot for cooking."

Now you have a problem. You have apples. The weaver wants a pot. You need to find a potter who wants apples, get a pot, and then go back to the weaver. But what if the potter does not want apples either? What if the potter wants fish? Now you need to find a fisherman who wants apples, trade for fish, take the fish to the potter, trade for a pot, and take the pot to the weaver. Just to get a blanket!

This is called **barter** — trading one thing directly for another. Barter works when both people happen to want what the other person has. But when they do not, it becomes a long, complicated chain of trades. The bigger the community, the harder barter becomes.

People figured this out thousands of years ago. And they invented something brilliant to solve it.

### Part 2: The Invention of Money

Money was invented to solve the barter problem. Instead of trading apples for blankets directly, everyone agreed to use one special thing as a go-between. The farmer could sell apples for money, and then use that money to buy a blanket from the weaver. The weaver could use that money to buy a pot. The potter could use that money to buy fish.

But what could that special thing be? People tried many different things throughout history:

- **Shells.** In many parts of the world, including Africa, Asia, and the Americas, people used cowrie shells as money. They were small, durable, and hard to find — which meant you could not just pick up unlimited amounts from the ground.
- **Salt.** The word "salary" actually comes from the Latin word for salt. Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt because it was valuable — everyone needed it to preserve food.
- **Cattle.** In ancient times, cows were money. The word "capital" (which means wealth) comes from the Latin word for "head" — as in head of cattle.
- **Metal coins.** Eventually, people discovered that metal — especially gold and silver — made the best money. Coins were small, did not rot or break, could be divided into different sizes, and were valuable enough that you did not need to carry a cart full of them.
- **Paper bills.** Much later, people invented paper money — a promise that the paper could be exchanged for a certain amount of gold or silver. Today, paper money is not backed by gold anymore, but people still accept it because the government says it is money and everyone agrees to treat it as money.

What makes something good money? It needs to be:
- **Durable** — it should not rot, break, or fall apart (this is why food makes bad money)
- **Portable** — you should be able to carry it easily (this is why giant stones make bad money)
- **Divisible** — you should be able to split it into smaller amounts for smaller purchases
- **Scarce** — there should not be an unlimited amount of it (if you could pick it up off the ground everywhere, it would not be valuable)
- **Accepted** — other people must be willing to take it in exchange for their goods and services

Money is a tool. Like a hammer or a saw, it does a specific job. Its job is to make exchange possible between any two people, even if they do not want the same things.

### Part 3: Where Does Value Come From?

Why does a glass of water cost almost nothing at home but might be worth a fortune in the middle of a desert?

Because **value comes from solving a problem.** In your house, water flows from the tap. There is no problem to solve. In the desert, a person is dying of thirst. Water solves the most urgent problem they have. The same water, in different situations, has very different value.

This is one of the most important ideas you will ever learn: **things are valuable because they help people.** A coat is valuable because someone is cold. A meal is valuable because someone is hungry. A ride to the airport is valuable because someone needs to catch a plane. A song is valuable because it makes someone happy.

If you want to understand money, start by understanding problems. Look around you. What do people need? What are they struggling with? What would make their lives easier, better, or more enjoyable? The person who can answer those questions — and then actually provide the solution — is the person who will always be able to earn money.

This is why **earning** money is different from **receiving** money. When you receive money as a gift or an allowance, you get the money but you do not practice seeing problems and solving them. When you earn money — by doing useful work, by making something someone wants, by providing a service that helps — you learn the connection between value creation and income. Both kinds of money spend the same way, but only earning teaches you the skill that will serve you for your entire life.

### Part 4: Spending Choices and Opportunity Cost

When you have five dollars and you spend it on a toy, that five dollars is gone. You cannot also spend it on a book, or save it for something bigger, or put it in your giving jar. Spending money on one thing means **not** spending it on everything else. This idea has a grown-up name: **opportunity cost.** The cost of buying the toy is not just five dollars — it is also the book you did not buy, the savings you did not add to, and the bigger item you could have saved toward.

This does not mean spending is bad. Spending is fine — it is what money is for! But spending without thinking means you never have money for the things that matter most to you. The person who spends every dollar the moment they get it always feels like they do not have enough. The person who stops and thinks — "Is this what I want most right now, or would I rather save for something better?" — that person feels in control.

### Part 5: Needs Versus Wants

Some things you need to survive: food, water, shelter, clothing, medicine when you are sick. These are **needs.** Without them, you are in real trouble.

Other things you enjoy but could live without: toys, candy, video games, entertainment, extra shoes beyond what you need. These are **wants.** Wants make life fun, but they are not required for survival.

The reason this distinction matters is not that wants are bad — wants are perfectly fine! The reason it matters is that **needs come first.** A family that spends all its money on fun things but cannot pay for food or shelter is in danger. A family that covers its needs first and then spends what is left on wants is secure.

As you get older, the line between needs and wants gets more interesting. Is a phone a need or a want? (It depends — for some people it is essential for work.) Is a car a need or a want? (It depends on where you live.) Learning to think about this honestly, without pretending wants are needs, is a skill that will save you from many problems.

### Part 6: Counting Money and Making Change

Money is real and physical, and you need to be able to handle it. Here are the basics:

**Coins:**
- A **penny** is worth 1 cent. It takes 100 pennies to make a dollar.
- A **nickel** is worth 5 cents. It takes 20 nickels to make a dollar.
- A **dime** is worth 10 cents. It takes 10 dimes to make a dollar.
- A **quarter** is worth 25 cents. It takes 4 quarters to make a dollar.

**Making change:** If something costs $3.75 and you pay with a $5 bill, your change is $1.25 — one dollar and one quarter, or five quarters, or one dollar, two dimes, and a nickel. There are many ways to make the same amount.

The best way to learn this is to **practice with real money in real situations.** When you go to a store, count out the money yourself. Verify the change you receive. When you save money, count your savings regularly. When you do a math lesson, use real coins to work the problems.

### Part 7: Fair Exchange — The Heart of All Commerce

A good trade is one where both people are happier afterward. You had something you valued less than what you received, and the other person had something they valued less than what they received. Both of you are better off. This is the heart of all fair exchange.

If you trade something and the other person feels tricked, that is not fair exchange. If you sell someone a cookie and it tastes terrible because you used bad ingredients, that is not fair exchange — even if you got their money. The person who cheats in a trade might gain in the short run, but they lose something more important: trust. And once trust is gone, nobody wants to trade with you anymore.

The best traders, the best business people, the best negotiators are the ones known for being fair. They get more opportunities, more customers, more trust, and more success over time — because people want to do business with someone they can trust.

This idea will follow you through every financial packet in this framework: **integrity is not just morally right — it is practically powerful.**

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Money Handling
**Developing well:** The child can count mixed coins accurately, make change, and verify transactions. They handle money comfortably and confidently. They might spontaneously calculate whether they have enough for something at a store.

**Developing slowly:** The child confuses coin values, cannot make change reliably, or shows no interest in the physical handling of money.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child had enough hands-on practice? Are they practicing with real transactions or only with exercises? Do they see you handling money and making purchasing decisions, or are all transactions done by card?

### 2. Understanding of Value
**Developing well:** The child can explain why something is valuable in terms of the problem it solves or the need it meets. They might say things like "Water is valuable because you need it to live" or "That tool is valuable because it helps Dad fix things." They do not simply say "it's valuable because it costs a lot."

**Developing slowly:** The child equates value with price — expensive things are "worth more." They cannot articulate why something is valuable beyond its cost.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you discussed value in terms of problem-solving, or only in terms of prices? When you make purchasing decisions, do you think aloud about value? ("This costs more, but it will last longer, so it is better value.")

### 3. Earning Versus Receiving
**Developing well:** The child understands and can articulate that earning money involves creating value for someone else. They have had the experience of earning money through a real value-creation task and can describe what they did and why someone was willing to pay for it.

**Developing slowly:** The child sees no difference between earning and receiving, or believes that earning just means "doing what an adult tells me to do."

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child actually earned money by creating value (not just completing assigned chores)? Have you helped them see the connection between the service they provided and the value it created for someone?

### 4. Opportunity Cost Awareness
**Developing well:** The child naturally considers alternatives before spending. They might say "If I buy this, I won't have enough for that." They can describe a real spending decision and what they gave up.

**Developing slowly:** The child spends impulsively with no consideration of alternatives, or wants to buy everything without any sense that choices involve trade-offs.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling decision-making aloud? ("I could buy this, but then I would not have enough for X. I think X matters more to me right now.") Is the child making real spending decisions with their own real money?

### 5. Fairness in Exchange
**Developing well:** The child understands that good trades leave both people happy. They object when a trade feels unfair (to themselves or to others). They connect fairness to trust — understanding that cheating in a trade damages future relationships.

**Developing slowly:** The child is either willing to take advantage of others in trades or has no opinion about fairness in exchange.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child participated in real trading situations? Have you discussed fairness after a trade or transaction? Does the child see you modeling fair dealing in your own transactions?
